title: Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow in Water? Fix It Fast
excerpt: Discover the most common reasons your monstera is turning yellow in water propagation and learn exactly how to fix it with our step-by-step troubleshooting guide.
content:
Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow in Water?

So you've set up a beautiful water propagation station, popped your monstera cutting in a glass vase, and now those gorgeous leaves are starting to turn an unsettling shade of yellow. Don't panic — this is one of the most common questions we get here at LeafLogic, and the good news is that it's almost always fixable.

Whether you're propagating a new cutting or growing your monstera hydroponically long-term, yellowing leaves are your plant's way of sending you a message. Let's decode exactly what it's trying to tell you.

The Most Common Reasons Your Monstera Is Yellowing in Water

1. Root Rot from Stagnant or Dirty Water

This is the number one culprit. When water sits without being refreshed, it becomes a breeding ground for bacteria and fungi. These microorganisms attack your monstera's developing roots, turning them brown and mushy — and that damage quickly shows up as yellow leaves.

  • What to look for: Slimy, dark-colored roots and cloudy, foul-smelling water
  • The fix: Change your water completely every 5–7 days, and rinse the container thoroughly each time
  • Pro tip: Use a clear glass or vase so you can monitor root health at a glance

2. Too Much Direct Sunlight

Monsteras love bright, indirect light — but direct sun combined with water can cause leaf scorch and trigger yellowing. The glass container also magnifies heat, essentially cooking your cutting from the outside in.

  • What to look for: Yellow or bleached patches on leaves that face the window
  • The fix: Move your cutting to a spot with bright, filtered light — think a few feet back from a south or west-facing window

3. Nutrient Deficiency

Plain tap water contains virtually no nutrients. While a fresh cutting can survive on stored energy for a while, over time your monstera will start showing signs of starvation — and yellow leaves are one of the first symptoms.

  • What to look for: Pale, evenly yellowing leaves, especially on newer growth
  • The fix: Add a diluted liquid hydroponic balanced liquid fertilizer to your water every two to three weeks. Use about one-quarter of the recommended dose to avoid fertilizer burn

4. Temperature Stress and Cold Water

Monsteras are tropical plants that thrive in warmth. If your water is too cold — especially if you're filling straight from the cold tap — it can shock the roots and cause yellowing. Similarly, a drafty windowsill in winter is a recipe for stressed, yellow leaves.

  • What to look for: Yellowing that appears after you changed the water, or during colder months
  • The fix: Let tap water sit for at least an hour before adding your cutting. Keep the ambient temperature between 65–85°F (18–29°C)

5. Chlorine and Fluoride Sensitivity

Tap water in many areas contains chlorine, chloramine, and fluoride — chemicals that are harmless to humans but can cause leaf tip yellowing and browning in sensitive plants like monsteras.

  • What to look for: Yellowing that starts at the leaf edges or tips
  • The fix: Switch to filtered water, distilled water, or let tap water sit uncovered overnight so chlorine can off-gas naturally

6. Leaves Submerged in Water

Only the stem and roots should be sitting in water — never the leaves or leaf nodes (other than the node you want to root). Submerged foliage decays rapidly, spreads bacteria, and quickly turns yellow and mushy.

  • What to look for: Yellow leaves that are partially or fully underwater
  • The fix: Trim your cutting so only the bare stem sits below the waterline. Remove any yellowed or decaying leaves immediately

How to Save a Yellowing Monstera in Water

  1. Remove the cutting from its container and inspect the roots and stem carefully.
  2. Trim any rotten or mushy roots with clean, sterilized scissors. Healthy roots should be white or light tan.
  3. Rinse the cutting and container thoroughly with clean water. A little white vinegar in the container can help eliminate bacteria.
  4. Refill with fresh, room-temperature filtered water and place the cutting back in, ensuring no leaves touch the water.
  5. Move to a spot with bright indirect light and a stable, warm temperature.
  6. Monitor closely for the next two weeks, changing the water weekly and watching for new root growth.

Preventing Yellow Leaves During Water Propagation

Prevention is always easier than cure. Follow these simple habits to keep your monstera thriving in water:

  • Change the water every 5–7 days without exception
  • Use a clean, clear vessel so you can spot problems early
  • Add a diluted liquid fertilizer once or twice a month
  • Keep your cutting in bright, indirect light away from cold drafts
  • Use filtered or pre-treated water to reduce chemical sensitivity
  • Make sure only the stem and roots are submerged — never the leaves

When Should You Move Your Monstera to Soil?

If your monstera has been struggling in water for several weeks and keeps producing yellow leaves despite your best efforts, it may simply be time to transition it to soil. Some monsteras are just happier in a well-draining quality potting mix.

Roots that develop in water are structurally different from soil roots, so transition gradually — start by mixing your potting medium with extra perlite to retain some moisture while the plant adjusts. Check out our complete guide on monstera propagation in soil vs. water for a full comparison of both methods.

Related Topics to Explore on LeafLogic

Yellowing leaves aren't unique to water propagation. If your potted monstera is also showing yellow leaves, the causes can overlap — overwatering, nutrient issues, and light problems all apply equally. Be sure to read our articles on why monstera leaves turn yellow in soil, our monstera watering guide, and our deep-dive into common monstera problems and how to fix them for even more troubleshooting help.

The Bottom Line

A yellowing monstera in water is almost always a sign of one of six fixable problems: dirty water, too much sun, nutrient deficiency, temperature stress, water quality issues, or submerged leaves. Start by changing the water, trimming any damaged roots, and reassessing your light and temperature setup — and in most cases, your monstera will bounce back beautifully.

Remember, monsteras are resilient plants. With a little detective work and consistent care, you'll be back to enjoying those stunning, healthy green leaves in no time.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why is my monstera turning yellow in water?

Yellowing is most commonly caused by stagnant or dirty water leading to root rot, but can also result from too much direct sunlight, nutrient deficiency, cold water shock, or sensitivity to chlorine and fluoride in tap water.

How often should I change the water for my monstera cutting?

Change the water completely every 5–7 days and rinse the container thoroughly each time to prevent bacterial and fungal buildup that causes root rot.

Do monsteras in water need fertilizer?

Yes, plain water contains virtually no nutrients, so add a diluted liquid hydroponic fertilizer every two to three weeks at about one-quarter of the recommended dose to prevent yellowing from nutrient deficiency.

What temperature water should I use for my monstera?

Avoid cold tap water, which can shock the roots. Let water sit for at least an hour before use and keep the ambient temperature between 65–85°F (18–29°C).

Can tap water cause my monstera leaves to turn yellow?

Yes, chlorine, chloramine, and fluoride in tap water can cause yellowing and browning, especially at leaf edges and tips. Switching to filtered or distilled water can resolve this issue.
